tree_topic: Games > Computer & Video Games > Gaming Media & Reference
questions: In the United States, sweepstakes promotions are subject to federal oversight to prevent unfair or deceptive practices. Identify the independent federal agency responsible for enforcing such consumer protection laws. Then, explain how its leadership structure is organized by law, including: how many commissioners compose the agency, how they are appointed and limited by political party affiliation, the length and staggering of their terms, and the conditions under which a commissioner may be removed. Additionally, describe how mid-20th-century presidential reorganization plans altered the distribution of executive and administrative functions within the agency, including which official received transferred powers and who was given the authority to designate the chair. Answer length: 220-320 words.

In the United States, sweepstakes promotions are regulated at the federal level by an independent agency tasked with preventing unfair or deceptive trade practices. Identify this agency and explain its original structure as established in 1914, including the number of commissioners, political party limitations, initial and subsequent term lengths, and grounds for removal. Additionally, describe how later presidential reorganization plans in 1950 and 1961 altered the internal distribution of executive, administrative, and delegatory powers within the agency, particularly regarding the role of the Chair and the delegation of functions.

In the United States, deceptive sweepstakes promotions (such as misleading prize notifications or false claims about odds of winning) are primarily policed by an independent federal agency created in the early 20th century. Identify this agency and provide a detailed explanation of: (1) how its leadership structure is organized, including the number of members, how they are appointed, political party limitations, term lengths, and how the chair is selected; and (2) the scope of its statutory authority to combat unfair or deceptive acts in commerce, including what types of conduct are declared unlawful, the agency’s power to issue cease-and-desist orders, and the availability of civil penalties or judicial enforcement when violations occur. Your answer should clearly connect the agency’s structural design with its enforcement powers as they relate to deceptive sweepstakes practices.

rounds.question: In the United States, sweepstakes promotions are subject to federal oversight to prevent unfair or deceptive practices. Identify the independent federal agency responsible for enforcing such consumer protection laws. Then, explain how its leadership structure is organized by law, including: how many commissioners compose the agency, how they are appointed and limited by political party affiliation, the length and staggering of their terms, and the conditions under which a commissioner may be removed. Additionally, describe how mid-20th-century presidential reorganization plans altered the distribution of executive and administrative functions within the agency, including which official received transferred powers and who was given the authority to designate the chair. Answer length: 220-320 words.
checklist_width: Agency consists of five Commissioners
Commissioners appointed by the President with advice and consent of the Senate
No more than three Commissioners may belong to the same political party
Commissioners serve seven-year terms (initial staggered terms of three to seven years)
Commissioners removable by the President for inefficiency, neglect of duty, or malfeasance in office
1950 Reorganization Plan transferred executive and administrative functions to the Chairman
1950 Reorganization Plan transferred authority to designate the Chairman to the President
1961 Reorganization Plan expanded delegation authority and transferred personnel assignment functions to the Chairman
checklist_depth: Federal Trade Commission (FTC) + Identified as the independent agency enforcing laws against unfair or deceptive practices affecting sweepstakes and consumer promotions
Reorganization Plan No. 8 of 1950 + Shifted executive/administrative powers to the Chairman and gave the President authority to designate the Chairman
Reorganization Plan No. 4 of 1961 + Granted delegation authority and further centralized personnel assignment functions under the Chairman
rounds.rationale: The question uses the sweepstakes domain as a logical entry point, requiring the respondent to identify the federal consumer protection agency that regulates deceptive promotions (Deep reasoning). It avoids naming the FTC directly, forcing identification through function. It then requires aggregation of multiple statutory and historical sources concerning the agency’s structure and two separate reorganization plans (Wide scope), ensuring multi-document synthesis rather than a single-source lookup.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Quick search by citation: 15 U.S. Code § 41 - Federal Trade Commission established; membership; vacancies; seal prev | next A commission is created and established, to be know
rounds.answer_a:
The independent federal agency responsible for enforcing consumer protection laws for sweepstakes promotions is the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) [1]. The FTC's leadership structure is defined by the Federal Trade Commission Act and subsequent amendments, establishing a commission of five members [2]. Commissioners are appointed by the President with Senate confirmation, and no more than three may belong to the same political party [3]. Each commissioner serves a seven-year term, with terms staggered so one term expires annually on September 26 [4]. Commissioners can only be removed by the President for "inefficiency, neglect of duty, or malfeasance in office" [5].

The 1950 Reorganization Plan No. 8 fundamentally restructured the FTC's operations [6]. This plan transferred the agency's administrative management and executive functions to a newly created Executive Director position, centralizing authority previously dispersed among bureau chiefs [7]. The plan also granted the President explicit authority to designate the Commission's Chair, a power previously exercised by the commissioners themselves [8]. These changes aimed to streamline operations and enhance accountability by separating policymaking from day-to-day administration [9]. The Chair, designated by the President, leads commission meetings and represents the agency publicly, while the Executive Director manages personnel, budget, and operational planning [10].

## References
[1] https://www.ftc.gov/about-ftc
[2] https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/15/41
[3] https://www.ftc.gov/system/files/documents/reports/annual-report-fy-2022/2022-FTC-Annual-Report.pdf
[4] https://www.congress.gov/bill/113th-congress/senate-bill/1828
[5] https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/15/46
[6] https://www.presidency.ucsb.edu/documents/reorganization-plan-no-8-1950
[7] https://fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL31983.pdf
[8] https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/1950/06/26/50-8159
[9] https://www.ftc.gov/system/files/documents/reports/ftc-105/history-ftc-105.pdf
[10] https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/15/41#f_1
rounds.answer_b:
The **Federal Trade Commission (FTC)** is the independent federal agency primarily responsible for enforcing sweepstakes consumer protection laws in the United States.[1][3] The FTC Act serves as the primary federal framework governing sweepstakes, prohibiting unfair or deceptive practices and requiring clear disclosure of material terms that would affect consumer participation decisions.[1]

The FTC's leadership structure is established by statute with the following organizational features: The agency is headed by **five commissioners** appointed by the President with Senate confirmation.[*] To ensure political balance, no more than three commissioners may be from the same political party.[*] Commissioners serve **seven-year terms that are staggered**, with one term expiring every year, ensuring continuity and limiting any single president's ability to control the agency through a complete turnover of leadership.[*] Commissioners may be removed by the President only **"for cause,"** protecting the agency's independence from partisan pressure and ensuring stability in enforcement priorities.[*]

Significant structural changes came through the Reorganization Plan No. 8 of 1950, which substantially altered the FTC's administrative framework.[*] This reorganization transferred considerable executive and administrative powers from the entire commission to the **Chairman**, who gained enhanced authority to direct agency operations and management.[*] The plan also granted the President explicit authority to **designate which commissioner would serve as Chair**, rather than leaving such selection to the commissioners themselves, thereby increasing presidential influence over the agency's operational direction while maintaining the commission's collegial decision-making authority over substantive regulatory and enforcement matters.[*]

These structural protections—particularly the party balance requirement, staggered terms, and removal "for cause" provisions—were designed to insulate the FTC from political pressures while enabling coordinated executive management through the designated Chair.

## References

[1] https://www.snipp.com/blog/quick-guide-to-sweepstakes-laws-and-rules

[3] https://revisionlegal.com/internet-law/online-contest-laws-business-compliance/

[*] Information regarding FTC organizational structure, commissioner appointment procedures, party affiliation limits, term lengths, removal provisions, and the Reorganization Plan of 1950.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identify the Federal Trade Commission (FTC), so both pass the DEEP logic check. On WIDTH/completeness, Agent A accurately describes the five commissioners, presidential appointment with Senate confirmation, party-balance limitation, seven-year staggered terms, and removal for inefficiency, neglect of duty, or malfeasance. However, Agent A makes a significant factual error regarding Reorganization Plan No. 8 of 1950, incorrectly stating that executive and administrative functions were transferred to an Executive Director rather than to the Chairman. Agent A also omits the 1961 Reorganization Plan’s expansion of delegation authority and transfer of personnel assignment functions to the Chairman. This constitutes a substantive WIDE failure. Agent B correctly explains that the 1950 Plan transferred executive and administrative powers to the Chairman and gave the President authority to designate the Chair. Agent B also accurately covers the commission structure, party limits, staggered seven-year terms, and for-cause removal. However, Agent B omits discussion of the 1961 Reorganization Plan, making it incomplete but not factually incorrect. In terms of user experience, Agent B provides clearer formatting (bolding, structured explanation) and a more accurate description of the reorganization changes. Because Agent A contains a material factual error and misses a required element, while Agent B is largely accurate but slightly incomplete, Agent B is the better answer.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

Identify this agency and explain its original structure as established in 1914, including the number of commissioners, political party limitations, initial and subsequent term lengths, and grounds for removal. Additionally, describe how later presidential reorganization plans in 1950 and 1961 altered the internal distribution of executive, administrative, and delegatory powers within the agency, particularly regarding the role of the Chair and the delegation of functions.

rounds.answer_a:
The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) is the independent agency regulating sweepstakes promotions under its authority to prevent unfair or deceptive trade practices [1]. Established by the Federal Trade Commission Act of 1914, the agency originally consisted of five commissioners, with no more than three from the same political party [2]. Commissioners were appointed by the President with Senate confirmation for initial terms of three, four, five, six, and seven years respectively to create staggered expiration, with subsequent terms standardized at seven years [3]. Removal was permitted only for "inefficiency, neglect of duty, or malfeasance in office" [4].

Reorganization Plan No. 8 of 1950 fundamentally restructured the FTC by transferring executive and administrative functions to a newly created Executive Director position, centralizing authority previously dispersed among bureau chiefs [5]. The plan also granted the President explicit authority to designate the Commission's Chair, shifting this power from the commissioners themselves [6]. The Chair would preside over meetings and represent the agency, while the Executive Director managed personnel, budget, and daily operations [7].

Reorganization Plan No. 4 of 1961 further refined the agency's structure by authorizing the Commission to delegate specific functions to individual commissioners or the Executive Director, enhancing operational flexibility [8]. This plan reinforced the separation between policymaking and administration, allowing the full Commission to focus on adjudication and rulemaking while empowering designated officials to execute routine matters [9]. The 1961 plan also clarified that the Chair, designated by the President, would continue leading the Commission publicly but would work collaboratively with the Executive Director on administrative matters [10].

## References
[1] https://www.ftc.gov/about-ftc
[2] https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/15/41
[3] https://www.ftc.gov/system/files/documents/reports/history-ftc/history-ftc-1914-2014.pdf
[4] https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/15/46
[5] https://www.presidency.ucsb.edu/documents/reorganization-plan-no-8-1950
[6] https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/1950/06/26/50-8159
[7] https://fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL31983.pdf
[8] https://www.presidency.ucsb.edu/documents/reorganization-plan-no-4-1961
[9] https://www.ftc.gov/system/files/documents/reports/ftc-105/history-ftc-105.pdf
[10] https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/15/41#f_1
rounds.answer_b:
The **Federal Trade Commission (FTC)** is the independent federal agency tasked with preventing unfair or deceptive trade practices, including those in sweepstakes promotions.[1]

The FTC was established in 1914 with **five commissioners** as its governing structure.[1] However, the provided search results do not contain specific details regarding the original 1914 statutory framework concerning political party affiliation requirements, initial term lengths, or formal removal provisions. The search results confirm that the FTC's leadership was established to prevent anticompetitive business practices and administer consumer protection laws, but lack the granular structural details about original commissioner appointment procedures and term specifications.[1]

The search results document that **Reorganization Plan No. 8 of 1950** significantly restructured the FTC's internal distribution of powers.[1] This reorganization transferred substantial executive and administrative authority from the collective commission to the **Chair position**, substantially altering the agency's operational hierarchy. The plan also established that the **President would designate which commissioner serves as Chair**, rather than allowing the commissioners to select their leader internally, thereby increasing presidential influence over administrative direction while preserving the commission's collegial authority over substantive regulatory matters.[1]

However, the provided search results contain no information regarding a 1961 reorganization plan or how it may have further modified the Chair's delegatory powers or the distribution of executive functions. This represents a significant gap in the available source material.

Regarding removal provisions, current FTC structure protects commissioners through "for cause" removal standards, though the search results do not explicitly detail whether this provision existed in the original 1914 statute or was established through subsequent legislation or reorganization plans.[1]

The search results provide limited historical detail about the FTC's foundational structure as enacted in 1914 and contain no information about 1961 reorganization effects, necessitating that complete answers to these specific questions would require access to additional sources beyond those provided.

## References

[1] https://kleinmoynihan.com/who-governs-sweepstakes-law/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) as the relevant independent agency overseeing unfair or deceptive trade practices, including sweepstakes enforcement. So both pass the core entity check.

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent A addresses nearly all checklist items—five commissioners, party limitation (no more than three from the same party), staggered initial terms (3–7 years), seven-year subsequent terms, removal for inefficiency/neglect/malfeasance, 1950 reorganization shifting executive/administrative authority and presidential designation of the Chair, and 1961 delegation authority. Although Agent A appears to misstate that executive functions were transferred to a newly created Executive Director (rather than primarily consolidating them in the Chair) and incompletely describes the 1961 delegation and review provisions, it still attempts to cover all required elements. 

Agent B, by contrast, omits most of the 1914 structural specifics (party limits, staggered terms, removal grounds), provides no substantive detail on the 1961 reorganization, and repeatedly defers due to lack of source material. This is a major failure of the WIDTH checklist.

Finally, User Experience: Agent A provides a structured, citation-rich, and comprehensive explanation that directly answers the query. Agent B reads as a partial refusal, offering minimal historical detail and little synthesis. From a search-agent perspective, Agent A clearly delivers a more complete and useful response.

Because Agent A contains some factual inaccuracies regarding internal restructuring details, it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER. However, it is decisively more complete and helpful than Agent B.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

Your answer should clearly connect the agency’s structural design with its enforcement powers as they relate to deceptive sweepstakes practices.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Quick search by citation: 15 U.S. Code § 41 - Federal Trade Commission established; membership; vacancies; seal prev | next A commission is created and established, to be know
rounds.answer_a:
The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) is the independent federal agency that polices deceptive sweepstakes promotions under its consumer protection authority [1]. Its leadership structure consists of five commissioners appointed by the President with Senate confirmation, with statutory limitations prohibiting more than three members from the same political party to ensure bipartisan oversight [2]. Commissioners serve seven-year staggered terms, with one term expiring annually on September 26, creating institutional continuity and shielding enforcement from abrupt political shifts [3]. The President designates one commissioner as Chair, who presides over meetings and serves as the agency's public representative, though the Chair possesses no greater voting power than other commissioners [4].

The FTC's statutory authority derives from Section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act, which declares unlawful "unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce" [5]. This explicitly covers sweepstakes deception such as false prize notifications, fabricated winning odds, undisclosed fees to claim prizes, and misrepresented prize values [6]. The agency can initiate administrative proceedings to issue cease-and-desist orders, compelling businesses to halt unlawful practices [7]. For ongoing or egregious violations, the FTC may pursue civil penalties of up to $50,120 per violation in federal court and obtain injunctive relief to immediately stop harmful activities [8]. The agency can also secure consumer redress and disgorgement of ill-gotten gains [9].

This structural design directly enhances enforcement efficacy against sweepstakes fraud. The bipartisan composition and staggered terms insulate the agency from political pressure, enabling consistent action across administrations. Collective decision-making by the full Commission ensures rigorous oversight of major enforcement actions, while the ability to delegate routine investigatory authority to staff enables rapid response to emerging deceptive sweepstakes schemes [10]. The Chair's presidential designation aligns agency priorities with executive policy while maintaining independent adjudication, creating a balanced framework that protects consumers from evolving sweepstakes fraud tactics.

## References
[1] https://www.ftc.gov/about-ftc
[2] https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/15/41
[3] https://www.ftc.gov/system/files/documents/reports/history-ftc/history-ftc-1914-2014.pdf
[4] https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/15/41#f_1
[5] https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/15/45
[6] https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2023/10/ftc-puts-sweepstakes-scammers-notice
[7] https://www.ftc.gov/enforcement/statutes/federal-trade-commission-act#15-usc-45
[8] https://www.ftc.gov/legal-library/browse/civil-penalty-adjustments
[9] https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/15/57b
[10] https://fas.org/sgp/crs/misc/RL31983.pdf
rounds.answer_b:
The **Federal Trade Commission (FTC)** is the independent federal agency primarily responsible for policing deceptive sweepstakes promotions in the United States.[1] Established in 1914, the FTC was originally created to prevent anticompetitive business practices and was subsequently granted authority over "deceptive acts or practices" and the administration of consumer protection laws.[1]

**Leadership Structure**

While the provided search results confirm the FTC's existence and general mandate, they do not contain detailed specifications regarding the precise number of commissioners, appointment procedures, political party limitations, term lengths, or chair selection mechanisms. The search results establish that the agency operates under a multi-member commission structure and reference a Reorganization Plan No. 8 of 1950 that transferred "considerable executive and administrative powers" to the Chair, with the President gaining authority to designate which commissioner serves as Chair.[1] However, comprehensive structural details are not fully articulated in these sources.

**Statutory Authority and Enforcement Powers**

The FTC's statutory authority encompasses prevention of "deceptive acts or practices" in commerce, with sweepstakes regulation as a significant enforcement domain.[1] Regarding sweepstakes specifically, the FTC requires that telemarketers "inform consumers of the nature or value of the prizes, that entering is free, and the terms and conditions to redeem a prize."[1] Marketing materials distributed through mail or Internet must "clearly disclose that consumers do not have to pay to participate."[1]

The search results document enforcement actions demonstrating the FTC's penalty authority. In June 2024, FTC settlements permanently banned sweepstakes scheme operators from operating sweepstakes or making prize claims, indicating cease-and-desist capabilities.[2] A 2019 settlement required operators of a sweepstakes scam to forfeit a record $30 million in cash and assets, demonstrating substantial civil penalty authority.[2]

The Deceptive Mail Prevention and Enforcement Act further grants the USPS authority to protect against deceptive sweepstakes mailings, with required disclosures characterized as "clearly and conspicuously displayed."[3] However, the provided search results lack detailed information about the formal cease-and-desist procedures, statutory penalty structures, and precise judicial enforcement mechanisms available to the FTC.

## References

[1] https://kleinmoynihan.com/who-governs-sweepstakes-law/

[2] https://www.ftc.gov/lottery-sweepstakes

[3] https://www.uspis.gov/wp-content/uploads/2019/12/pub-546_consumers-guide-to-sweepstakes-lotteries_508.pdf
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identify the Federal Trade Commission (FTC), so both pass the DEEP logic check. However, Agent A comprehensively satisfies the WIDTH checklist: it correctly explains the FTC’s 1914 creation, five-member structure, presidential appointment with Senate confirmation, bipartisan limit (no more than three from one party), seven-year staggered terms, presidential designation of the Chair, Section 5 authority declaring unfair or deceptive acts unlawful, administrative complaints and hearings, cease-and-desist orders, civil penalties, and federal court injunctions. It also clearly links structural independence (bipartisan design, staggered terms) to enforcement effectiveness against deceptive sweepstakes. 

Agent B, by contrast, omits most required structural details (number of commissioners, party limitations, term lengths, Senate confirmation) and fails to explain core enforcement mechanics such as formal cease-and-desist procedures, judicial review in Courts of Appeals, and statutory civil penalty mechanisms. Instead of synthesizing well-known statutory structure, it defers due to “lack of search results,” resulting in major checklist gaps. This is a clear WIDTH failure. 

On user experience, Agent A is far more complete, better grounded, and directly answers every component of the prompt with strong explanatory linkage. Agent B is sparse and incomplete. Therefore, Agent A is MUCH_BETTER.
